turns.content: I'm trying to get PGO right in my Rust project but I keep getting confused about where the LLVM profiles actually come from. Can you walk me through how cargo pgo works?

The cargo pgo crate is basically a wrapper around the underlying LLVM infrastructure, so it handles the heavy lifting for you. It runs your binary with instrumentation enabled to generate trace data that gets collected into profile information files using llvm-profdata tools. The key thing is that this needs to happen before optimization, otherwise you're just wasting cycles on suboptimal builds.
So I need to compile with some special flags first? What exactly does the instrumentation flag look like?

You definitely need to enable LLVM instrumentation during that initial build so the compiler can track function entry and exit points. The cargo pgo crate automatically manages this, but if you're doing it manually with rustc, you'd use -C instrument-coverage or similar flags depending on your target triple. This generates the raw trace data that llvm-profdata later aggregates into a binary profile format.
Once I have those profiles merged, what's the actual build command to apply them?

After merging your profile files with llvm-profdata, you rebuild using -Cprofile-use on your target triple. This tells LLVM's optimizer to use that specific profile data when deciding which code paths are hot spots. The cargo pgo crate simplifies this by running the whole pipeline for you in a single command.
That sounds convenient, but what if I want more control? Can I manually merge profiles before using them?

Yes, you can absolutely do that manually. You run llvm-profdata -merge on your collected profile files to create a single binary profile object, then pass it to rustc with the -Cprofile-use flag pointing to that merged file. This gives you granular control over which profiles get applied and how they're combined.
I'm curious about the difference between PGO and LTO, do I need both?

They serve different purposes but often work together. LTO does cross-crate optimizations by merging object files, while PGO uses runtime data to guide inlining and dead code elimination. For maximum performance on complex libraries, you typically want both - compile with LTO flags for inter-crust optimization and then apply PGO profiles afterward.
What about the cargo pgo crate specifically? Does it handle anything else besides the basic workflow?

The cargo pgo crate also handles cleaning up temporary files and managing multiple profile runs, which is helpful when testing different build configurations. It integrates with Cargo's build system so you can chain it into your normal release pipeline without much extra setup code.
One last thing - what if my binary size goes up after applying PGO?

That shouldn't happen normally since PGO removes dead code and improves inlining, but you might see minor increases due to better function growth tables or alignment changes. Usually the runtime performance gains far outweigh any few kilobytes of extra size. If it's a major issue, check your profile data quality - bad profiles can actually hurt optimization.

I'm trying to port our existing C++ codebase to Rust, but the binary is still running slow because the optimizer didn't guess my control flow correctly. I tried using cargo pgo and LLVM instrumentation but it's just adding noise. My current setup looks like this: I compile with -fprofile-generate, run a test suite, then try to rebuild with -Cprofile-use, yet the performance gain is negligible compared to what we saw in C++. Why does PGO feel so fragile when porting?

The fragility comes from how LLVM merges and applies profile data. When you generate profiles with -fprofile-generate, LLVM instruments your functions to count basic block execution frequencies. These counts get collected into a binary format that llvm-profdata understands, but the real magic happens when you rebuild with -Cprofile-use. The compiler reads this merged profile information to make decisions about inlining, loop unrolling, and register allocation. If your Rust test suite doesn't match the C++ workload's execution path, the optimizer will optimize wrong paths instead of hot paths, effectively degrading performance rather than improving it.

That makes sense about matching workloads, but I'm confused about how exactly the merging works. We're running cargo pgo which seems to handle this automatically. Does it actually invoke llvm-profdata correctly? Or am I missing a step where I need to manually combine my profile data before passing it to the compiler?

cargo pgo does handle the merging, but you need to understand what it's actually doing under the hood. It compiles your binary with -fprofile-generate, runs your tests, then uses llvm-profdata to merge all those profile files into a single archive before rebuilding with -Cprofile-use. The key is that PGO relies on LLVM understanding the Rust ABI and calling conventions correctly during the instrumentation phase. If you're mixing multiple crates or using unsafe code blocks incorrectly in your test suite, the instrumented functions might not match expectations, leading to garbage data being merged into your final profile file.

So if my Rust code has some unsafe blocks that don't behave exactly like C++ intrinsics, that could corrupt the whole optimization pass? Like when I'm using raw pointers or foreign function interfaces in my tests?

Yes, exactly. When you have unsafe code with raw pointers or FFI calls, the LLVM optimizer might misinterpret control flow transitions because it doesn't know how Rust's memory safety guarantees interact with low-level optimizations. The PGO process assumes a consistent calling convention and data layout for every instrumented function call. If your test suite exercises functions that cross boundaries between safe and unsafe regions in ways the compiler didn't expect, the profile counts become unreliable. You might want to isolate your PGO tests to pure Rust code paths first before worrying about FFI or raw pointer usage.

Okay, so my strategy should be: run a clean test suite without any unsafe blocks, collect profiles, then gradually introduce complexity? But what if our actual production code has tons of unsafe blocks and FFI calls?

You're right to think that way. The solution is incremental PGO: start with a subset of your hot paths in safe Rust, generate profiles for those specifically, then rebuild the entire project using those specific profile files. You can use llvm-profdata --merge to select only the relevant sections from your test output rather than relying on cargo pgo's default behavior which might include too much noise. Once you've optimized the critical path with accurate data, you can then add unsafe code and FFI calls back in without breaking the optimization assumptions.

I totally understand why I am struggling with Rust right now, but honestly it feels like the toolchain is just throwing random flags at me without any logic. Why does this even work this way where we have to manually collect profiles and then merge them before rebuilding? It seems so convoluted compared to how normal compilers just optimize automatically.

That frustration is very common when people first encounter PGO. The reason it feels manual is that LLVM's optimization passes need concrete data about which code paths are actually taken in your program to make smart decisions, and the standard compiler flow doesn't automatically generate this for every single binary. With Rust specifically, we use crates like cargo pgo or llvm-profdata to collect runtime execution traces from instrumented binaries and then merge those profiles into the LLVM bitcode before compilation with -Cprofile-use.

Okay so I get that we need data, but my understanding is that once we run our program once to generate the profile file, we just add that flag and it magically knows exactly how fast everything should be. Is it really necessary to rebuild the entire crate from scratch or can we just patch the existing binary?

You are making a mistake here by assuming the compiler can apply those optimizations on top of an already compiled object file without recompilation. The -Cprofile-use flag tells the Rustc compiler to re-optimize its internal IR based on your merged profile data, which fundamentally changes how it generates machine code for functions like std::mem::swap or core::ptr::read. You cannot patch a binary because the optimization passes operate on the intermediate representation before it becomes assembly; you must rebuild the entire crate with the new flags applied to ensure the final machine code reflects the PGO guidance.

Wait, so if I just run cargo build and then cargo pgo collect, won't that automatically handle all the LLVM instrumentation for me? I don't need to manually instrument my source files with any special macros or traits?

You are right about not needing manual instrumentation; crates like cargo pgo do generate the necessary LLVM instrumentation code internally using a trait that wraps your functions. However, there is still one crucial step you cannot skip: after collecting those profiles, you must explicitly merge them into a single profile file using llvm-profdata, and then pass that merged file to rustc via -Cprofile-use. If you skip this merge step or forget the flag, PGO will simply not apply its optimizations because the compiler never sees the consolidated execution data.

This is getting quite deep so I think I need to understand exactly what happens inside rustc when it processes that merged profile file. Can you explain how the Rust compiler actually reads those profiles and changes its optimization passes?

When rustc sees -Cprofile-use, it intercepts its internal IR generation pipeline before the final code emission phase. Instead of relying on static assumptions about function call frequencies, the Rust compiler reads the merged profile data and uses that information to guide its inlining decisions and loop optimizations specifically for hot paths like your main loop or critical sections in std::collections. For example, if a particular function is called 90% of the time based on your PGO profiles, rustc will aggressively inline that function into surrounding code even if it normally wouldn't.

That makes sense for inlining but what about memory access patterns? Does PGO actually help me optimize the cache behavior of my data structures like Vec or HashMap since those are often the bottleneck?

Yes, PGO significantly helps with memory access patterns by influencing how the compiler allocates stack frames and manages pointers to your Vec or HashMap internals. When rustc optimizes based on real execution traces from llvm-profdata, it can reorder instructions and allocate data structures in a way that minimizes cache misses for frequently accessed elements. While you cannot directly control these low-level memory layouts with Rust traits alone, the PGO process effectively teaches the compiler how your specific access patterns behave so it can generate more efficient machine code.

I think I see now why this is useful but I am still confused about why we have to go through all these LLVM steps instead of just using Rust's built-in optimization. Is there a fundamental difference between how the standard optimizer works versus PGO?

The standard optimizer relies on static analysis and heuristics that assume average usage patterns, whereas PGO uses dynamic profiling data to make informed decisions about what actually matters in your specific application. For a Rust crate with complex interop or highly tuned performance needs like embedded systems, the static assumptions often miss critical bottlenecks. The LLVM toolchain combined with rustc allows you to inject real-world execution traces into the compilation process so that functions like core::ptr::copy are optimized specifically for how your program actually uses memory rather than how it might theoretically use it.
what's the difference between PGO and regular optimization in Rust?

Regular optimizations rely on general heuristics to guess how code is used, whereas Profile-Guided Optimization uses actual runtime data collected from your specific build. Think of it like cooking: standard optimization is a recipe that assumes everyone eats similarly, while PGO is tasting the food before serving and adjusting the seasoning based on what you actually prefer. In Rust terms, regular optimizations use generic assumptions about function call patterns, but PGO collects trace information using LLVM instrumentation to merge profiles with llvm-profdata and then rebuilds with -Cprofile-use flags so the compiler knows exactly which paths matter.
I've heard of cargo pgo crate, does it handle everything automatically?

The cargo pgo crate simplifies the process by managing the collection phase for you. It runs your binary to gather execution traces using LLVM instrumentation, then merges those profiles with llvm-profdata before rebuilding with -Cprofile-use flags. However, you still need to ensure the binary is compiled without PGO initially so it can generate fresh profile data rather than overwriting itself.
What happens if I run cargo pgo twice in a row?

Running cargo pgo twice will cause issues because it tries to overwrite existing profiles with llvm-profdata. The first run collects the necessary execution traces, but subsequent runs attempt to merge already-collected data which can corrupt the profile information or lead to inconsistent optimization results.
Can I use PGO on a library crate instead of a binary?

Yes, you can apply PGO to library crates by treating them as if they were binaries during the profiling phase. You would build your library with -Cprofile-generate flags to collect data via LLVM instrumentation, then merge those profiles using llvm-profdata before rebuilding with -Cprofile-use flags so the compiler uses the collected statistics for optimization decisions.
Does this apply to Rust's standard library too?

The standard library itself is not typically optimized using PGO because it must remain compatible across many different use cases. However, you can opt into PGO for your own crates that depend on the standard library by ensuring your build configuration doesn't interfere with shared symbols when merging profiles with llvm-profdata.
Why do I need to disable some features before running cargo pgo?

You need to disable certain features because PGO requires a clean state where the compiler can accurately collect execution traces using LLVM instrumentation. Some features might interfere with the profiling process or prevent the merge step from working correctly when rebuilding with -Cprofile-use flags.
What if my binary is too large and slow to run for profiling?

For large binaries, you can profile specific modules instead of the entire program. This allows you to focus on critical paths without waiting for the whole process to complete. You would compile individual components with -Cprofile-generate flags so LLVM instrumentation only instruments those parts when merging profiles with llvm-profdata.
How does this affect my release build performance?

Once you merge profiles with llvm-profdata and rebuild with -Cprofile-use flags, your release build will show significant improvements in critical paths. The compiler uses the collected statistics to make smarter decisions during optimization, resulting in better code generation for the actual usage patterns of your application.

I keep reading about PGO and how it can make binaries faster, but I still don't fully understand why my current build isn't taking advantage of it. It feels like the compiler is just guessing which code paths are hot when I haven't actually run any benchmarks against real-world usage data.

That's a great intuition to start with, but there's a common misconception here: PGO doesn't magically know your application's behavior; it relies entirely on LLVM instrumentation to record execution counts for specific functions during runtime. If you skip the profiling phase and jump straight into optimization without collecting actual data, the compiler will optimize based on generic assumptions rather than your real workload.
So I should just run my app a few times and then rebuild? That sounds too easy compared to what I've been told about needing complex tools.

You're right that it's simpler than you might think, but there are specific steps required: first, compile with LLVM instrumentation enabled so the runtime tracks function entry/exit counts; then use llvm-profdata to merge those trace files into a single profile database; finally, rebuild your code using -Cprofile-use or via the cargo pgo crate. Skipping any of these means you won't get the performance gains.
I've tried adding the flags manually but my binary is still slow. Is it possible I'm missing something in how Rust handles this compared to C++?

Rust does handle PGO similarly, but you need to ensure your build script properly invokes cargo pgo or sets the appropriate environment variables before running cargo build; otherwise the compiler won't apply the optimized instruction scheduling based on your merged profiles. Also remember that Rust's -Cprofile-use flag must come after all other optimization flags in your command line.
Wait, so if I don't merge the profiles correctly with llvm-profdata, the compiler just ignores them? That seems like a huge waste of time.

Exactly. If you skip merging or use incompatible flags, the optimizer receives empty or malformed profile data and defaults to standard optimizations instead of PGO-specific ones. You can verify this by checking if your binary size drops significantly after rebuilding with correct profiles; otherwise something went wrong in the pipeline.
I think I finally get it now, but I'm worried about debugging issues later since PGO changes how instructions are arranged. Does that make testing harder?

Not really. While PGO does reorder basic blocks for performance, the resulting binary is still fully compatible with standard Rust debug and release modes; you can still use cargo test or clippy without issues. The key is ensuring your unit tests cover enough of the hot paths so the profile data remains accurate.
One last thing: what happens if I run PGO on a library that's meant to be shared across multiple projects? Will it cause problems?

That's actually ideal for PGO because shared libraries benefit greatly from optimized instruction scheduling. Just make sure you profile the final combined build rather than individual components, so all inter-procedural optimizations are captured in one go.

I'm trying to get my Rust build faster and I've been told that just compiling with the default optimization flags isn't enough. Can you explain the core idea behind PGO and why it actually matters for a real-world binary?

Great question. At its heart, PGO is about letting your program teach the compiler what paths are important by running it in profile mode first. When LLVM compiles Rust code into machine instructions, it makes decisions based on frequency data from previous runs; if you don't give it that context, it often picks a generic path instead of an efficient one. The toolchain uses this to produce binaries that run significantly faster because the compiler can choose the best instruction sequence for actual usage patterns.
Okay so I need to actually collect data from my application before compiling? What does that process look like and what specific steps do I take with LLVM?

Exactly, you first run your application in profile-generate mode. This step uses LLVM instrumentation to record how often different functions are called and which code paths get executed. You then merge those collected profiles using llvm-profdata into a single binary format that the toolchain can understand. Once you have that combined data file, the compiler reads it during compilation with -Cprofile-use to make smarter choices about inlining and instruction selection.
That sounds like a lot of extra steps though. Is there something simpler I should use instead of manually managing llvm-profdata for every build?

You are right that manual workflow is tedious, so the cargo pgo crate abstracts most of this away by handling profile collection and merging internally. However, you still need to understand the underlying concept because without PGO, your Rust binaries might not hit their performance potential even with great code quality.
So if I just add that crate and run the command, will my release binary automatically be optimized?

Not exactly; you must explicitly invoke cargo pgo to generate the profile data before running your normal build with -Cprofile-use. Think of it as a two-step process where the first step collects the intelligence about how your program behaves in production-like conditions, and then the second step uses that information to compile an optimized binary.

I just tried to compile my binary with the PGO flag enabled and it seems faster than before, but I thought I was doing something wrong because I didn't collect any profiles first. Can someone explain why this compilation fails or if there is a better way to handle this?

The issue you're facing comes from misunderstanding how the compiler processes your input arguments when trying to use PGO without prior profile data. When you pass -Cprofile-use, the LLVM backend expects a specific format for the profile data that matches what was collected earlier with llvm-profdata merge. If you skip this step or provide invalid binary profiles, the optimizer cannot apply the correct heuristics to your Rust code's call graph and type information. You need to understand that PGO relies on actual runtime frequency data from real executions before it can optimize your crate correctly.
Oh, so I was just guessing? But wait, isn't cargo pgo supposed to handle this automatically for me? Why am I still getting errors?

Cargo pgo is indeed designed to automate the entire workflow, but it only works if your build environment has LLVM instrumentation enabled in the first place. If you're using a standard Rust toolchain without enabling the debug assertions or specific compiler flags that trigger the profiling hooks, cargo pgo cannot generate the necessary binary profiles from scratch. The crate expects your compilation to include -C instrument-coverage or similar flags during the initial build so it can capture function call frequencies before merging them with llvm-profdata.
That makes sense, but I still don't understand why my program wasn't optimized properly even after trying to run cargo pgo. Is there a way to manually do this without relying on the crate?

You can manually handle PGO by first building your project with -C instrument-coverage to generate coverage data, then using llvm-profdata merge to combine those outputs into a single profile file. Once you have that merged profile, you rebuild your application with the -Cprofile-use flag pointing to your custom data. This gives you full control over which binaries are analyzed and how they're optimized, though it requires more manual steps compared to using cargo pgo.
Thanks for clarifying! I think I finally get why the compiler was throwing errors. But what exactly happens inside LLVM when it sees those profile arguments?

When LLVM sees the -Cprofile-use flag, it loads your merged binary profiles and uses them to guide its optimization passes during compilation. The compiler analyzes your Rust code's type system and call graph against the collected frequencies to make smarter decisions about inlining, dead code elimination, and instruction selection. Without proper profile data, these optimizations cannot run effectively because they lack context about which functions are actually called most often.

I'm building a high-throughput data processing service and I want to squeeze every drop of performance out of it, but I keep thinking PGO is just about running the binary once with some special flags. If I compile my main crate with -Cprofile-use and assume the default profile will magically capture all runtime behavior, won't that be enough for a standard library like serde_json?

That's a common misconception, but it misses the point. The PGO flow isn't just about compiling with -Cprofile-use; you actually need to collect runtime profiles first using LLVM instrumentation on your actual workloads. Then you merge those binaries into a single profile file using llvm-profdata-merge before rebuilding.
Right, so I should just run cargo pgo and it handles everything automatically without me needing to manually instrument the code?

The cargo-pgo crate does automate a lot of this for you by wrapping the entire pipeline. It will run your binary, collect traces via LLVM instrumentation, merge them with llvm-profdata-merge, and then rebuild with -Cprofile-use in one go.
That sounds convenient, but I'm worried about the overhead of running that extra build cycle. Won't it slow down my development workflow significantly if I have to wait for profiles every time?
You're right to be concerned about the setup cost. The profile collection can take minutes depending on your workload, especially for large binaries like a full system crate.
So is it worth it if I just run cargo pgo once and then never change my code? Can I skip re-collecting profiles after every minor tweak?
Exactly. Once you have your merged profile file, you can reuse it across many builds using -Cprofile-use without needing to re-run the instrumentation pipeline.
I see, so I only need to run cargo pgo once at the start of my project? But what if I add a new hot path later that wasn't in the original binary execution?

That's when you should re-run cargo pgo with your updated code to update the profiles. The optimizer will only know about paths it sees during profile collection, so missing hot paths means missed optimizations.

Got it, but I'm still confused about the difference between -Cprofile-use and just compiling normally with optimization flags enabled. Does PGO actually enable parallelism that normal compilation doesn't?

Normal optimizations might help a bit, but PGO with LLVM's LTO passes can enable aggressive inlining and vectorization specifically tuned to your actual execution patterns. It often unlocks parallelism opportunities that the compiler missed when running on generic code.
Alright, so I should probably avoid assuming my default build is optimal and just start using cargo pgo from day one instead of waiting until performance issues appear?
Absolutely. It's better to pay the upfront cost now than to debug performance issues later when you've spent time writing suboptimal code.
